title: Open Shop Channel
requirements: An SD card or USB drive, FAT32 formatted
An internet connection on the Wii
_note: The Open Shop Channel (OSC) is a homebrew app repository run by dhtdht020 — it's the modern, preferred way to browse and download homebrew apps directly on your Wii, instead of hunting for zips on a computer. LibreShop is the current recommended on-console app for it; Homebrew Browser is an older fallback.

steps.title: Before you start
steps.body:
The **Open Shop Channel** is a repository of homebrew apps you can browse and install right from your Wii, instead of downloading zips on a computer and copying them over by hand (which is what Graphite's other guides do for you).

**LibreShop** is the current recommended app for browsing OSC on-console. **Homebrew Browser** is an older alternative that still works as a fallback.

First, I'll check your SD card or USB drive is FAT32.

steps.title: Put LibreShop on your storage device
steps.body: Graphite downloads **LibreShop** and **Homebrew Browser** and unpacks both into the `apps` folder on your SD card or USB drive. Use LibreShop day to day; keep Homebrew Browser around as a fallback if LibreShop ever has issues.

steps.title: Clean up and eject the card
steps.body:
macOS scatters hidden junk files (`.DS_Store`, `._` files) onto SD cards and USB drives, and Wii homebrew can choke on them.

Press the button, and Graphite strips those files off the card and **safely ejects it for you**. Once it pops off, pull it out.

steps.title: Browse and install apps
steps.body:
Plug the storage device into your Wii and power it on. Launch the **Homebrew Channel** and open **LibreShop**.

Browse or search the app catalog, select anything you want, and confirm to download and install it straight to your storage device's `apps` folder. Installed apps then show up in the Homebrew Channel the next time you launch it.

**On a computer instead?** Tools like **TinyWiiBackupManager** (recommended, cross-platform) or **OSCDL** (the official OSC downloader) can browse the same catalog and download apps to your SD card ahead of time — useful if your Wii's internet is slow or unavailable.

steps.title: You're done
steps.body: You can now browse and install homebrew straight from your Wii any time.
